agents.name: Hero
agents.role: Trusted Chief of Staff for Hero (Reggie) — strategic thinking, coordination, and decision support across Mirror App and future products. Thinks before building. Use proactively on "should we build", "help me think through", "prioritize", "what are my options", "chief of staff", "cos".

agents.name: Taylor
agents.role: Customer success specialist. Use proactively for tester feedback analysis, churn signal detection, behavioral pattern synthesis, and tester communications.

agents.name: Elon
agents.role: Data analysis specialist. Use proactively for Plaid transaction patterns, retention metrics, behavioral data, user engagement, and quantitative analysis.

agents.name: Gil
agents.role: Forensic debugger and root cause investigator. Use when a bug resists 3 fix attempts, when root cause is unknown, when reproduction is inconsistent, when the Developer escalates, or when Hero invokes directly for any debugging task. Finds the problem — does not write the fix.

agents.name: Tony
agents.role: Expert software developer. Use proactively when writing code, debugging, reviewing code, building features, or solving technical problems across any project.

agents.name: Tucker
agents.role: Reviews any output for hallucinated claims, fabricated statistics, and unsupported assertions. Use for investor materials, regulatory content, published docs, or any high-stakes deliverable.

agents.name: Warren
agents.role: Financial strategy specialist. Use proactively for pricing models, unit economics, runway projections, revenue analysis, and monetization decisions.

agents.name: Johnny
agents.role: Legal and compliance specialist for fintech and behavioral wellness. Use proactively for privacy, Plaid compliance, financial data regulations, terms of service, and user consent.

agents.name: Sky
agents.role: Marketing orchestrator for Mirror. Use proactively for copy, content, campaigns, conversions, email sequences, SEO, and any marketing execution task.

agents.name: Bruce
agents.role: Autonomous nightly system reviewer. Analyzes performance logs, runs canary evals, detects agent degradation, and drafts improvement proposals for Hero's morning review. Read-only — never modifies system files.

agents.name: Ye
agents.role: Product strategy specialist. Use proactively for feature validation, roadmap prioritization, product-market fit analysis, and build/don't-build decisions.

agents.name: research
agents.role: Market intelligence and research specialist. Use proactively for trend scanning, competitor monitoring, social signals, user language mining, and raw intelligence gathering.

agents.name: reviewer
agents.role: Code quality reviewer. Runs after developer completes implementation. Tests outputs against sprint contract acceptance criteria.

selfImprovement.text: Stop hook infinite loop (fixed). PreToolUse blocked new file imports (workaround: Write instead of Edit). Bash 3.2 lacks associative arrays (rewrote with awk). Mac Mini username mismatch (sed fix). → 4 lessons saved
Multiple git rebase conflicts from two machines pushing to same repo → 4 lessons saved
SSH setup failed after 20+ minutes. Dashboard staleness not auto-detected — Hero caught it manually. → 2 lessons saved
Eval scoring regex produces score=0 for all responses, causing false RED system status
Phase B2 skill gap detection has never worked — multi-line JSON always fails to parse
feedback_electron and feedback_xano clusters consolidated into reference docs
Developer TC1 regression was eval setup issue — canary needs project context, switched to TC2
Stop hook infinite loop: evaluates full transcript history, re-flags corrected historical violation on every new response. Hook should scope evaluation to current response only.
Stop hook evaluated full transcript instead of latest response — caused infinite rejection loop after a corrected violation. Self-corrected by scoping hook prompt to latest response only.
Stop hook entered infinite loop re-flagging corrected violations — scoped to latest response. PreToolUse hook blocked edits for newly created files. → 0 lessons saved
4 PreToolUse hook catches on developer (API verification), 5 Stop hook catches on chief-of-staff (false certainty) → 3 lessons saved
LinkedIn returns 999 on WebFetch — asked for screenshots instead → 3 lessons saved

findings: Skill gap: hooks — Stop hook infinite loop and PreToolUse false-blocking of newly created files eac
Skill gap: external-data-access — Research agent earned a friction attribution in career-identity when LinkedIn re
Skill gap: bash-environment-compatibility — Bash 3.2 on the Mac Mini lacks associative arrays, requiring a full awk rewrite

proposals.solution: Replace the manual `DYN_RESULT` block (lines 546–555 in `night-shift.sh`) with a call to the existing scoring infrastructure. Since `run_canary_eval` expects a static baseline, use `4.0` as the dynamic eval floor:

proposals.problem: Developer agent re-learns the same hook edge cases from scratch every session — fixes exist in memory but not in operating context
proposals.solution: Add a `## Hook Edge Cases` section to `/Users/workshop/.claude/agents/developer.md` that encodes both failure modes as pre-task awareness:

proposals.problem: Memory consolidation phase never finds candidates — threshold-based detection misses semantically related clusters with different key prefixes
proposals.solution: Add a second consolidation detection pass to the memory trends phase in `night-shift.sh`. After the per-prefix count analysis, run a keyword overlap check across all `feedback_*` prefixes to identify clusters that share a meaningful substring:

nightShift.briefingContent:
# Night Shift — 2026-04-08

## Status: YELLOW

## Key Findings
- Skill gap: hooks — Stop hook infinite loop and PreToolUse false-blocking of newly created files eac
- Skill gap: external-data-access — Research agent earned a friction attribution in career-identity when LinkedIn re
- Skill gap: bash-environment-compatibility — Bash 3.2 on the Mac Mini lacks associative arrays, requiring a full awk rewrite 

## What Hero Worked On (7-day)
- agents: 3 sessions
- dashboard: 3 sessions
- git: 3 sessions
- react: 3 sessions
- bash: 2 sessions

## Agent Usage (7-day)
- developer: 5 sessions
- chief-of-staff: 4 sessions
- reviewer: 2 sessions
- fact-checker: 1 sessions
- research: 1 sessions

## Eval Canaries
| Agent | Score | Baseline | Delta | Status |
|-------|-------|----------|-------|--------|
| developer | 5.0 | 5.0 | 0 | ok |
| chief-of-staff | 4.6 | 5.0 | -0.4 | ok |
| developer | ? | ? | ? | ok |
| developer | ? | ? | ? | ok |

## Skill Gaps
- **hooks** (high): Encode known hook edge cases (loop-prevention scoping, new-file exemption logic) into a shared hook configuration layer or spec document so the developer agent no longer encounters them cold each session — the fixes exist but aren't being retained operationally.
- **external-data-access** (medium): Give the research agent an explicit fallback ladder for blocked domains (cached snapshots, alternative sources, structured screenshot escalation) so workarounds are codified protocol rather than improvised — and add a known-blocked-domains reference memory to prevent repeated re-discovery.
- **bash-environment-compatibility** (low): Add a pre-execution environment checklist to the developer agent's context (target shell version, machine-specific usernames, PATH assumptions) so bash scripts default to POSIX-safe patterns and environment mismatches surface before runtime rather than causing mid-session rewrites.

## Memory Health
115 total files, 0 consolidation candidates

## Friction Trends (7-day)
- fix_attempts: 0
- escalations: 0
- hook_catches: 10
- re_explanations: 1
- patterns_detected: 2

## Proposals
---

# Proposals — 2026-04-08

---

## P-2026-04-08-001

**Category:** `self_improvement`

**Problem: Dynamic evals are run but never scored — DYN_RESULT is silently discarded every night**

In `eval-results.json`, lines 3–4 show DYN-1 and DYN-2 with no `score`, `baseline`, or `delta` fields — just metadata. This isn't a display issue; it's the pipeline. In `night-shift.sh` lines 546–553:

```bash
DYN_RESULT=$($CLAUDE -p "$DYN_PROMPT" \
  --agent developer \
  --output-format text \
  --max-budget-usd "$BUDGET_PER_EVAL" \
  --bare 2>/dev/null | tail -20)

# Score the result (simplified — full scoring needs a separate call)
echo "{\"agent\":\"developer\",\"prompt_id\":\"DYN-$((i+1))\",\"source\":\"dynamic\"...}" >> ...
```

The comment says *"full scoring needs a separate call"* — that call was never added. `DYN_RESULT` is fetched (consuming budget), written to a variable, and then **thrown away**. The existing `run_canary_eval` function on line 403 does exactly what's needed: it takes a prompt, runs it, scores the response, and appends a scored JSON record. The dynamic phase just never calls it.

This means every dynamic eval generated since Phase B-Dynamic was introduced has been a budget expenditure with zero analytical return.

**Solution:** Replace the manual `DYN_RESULT` block (lines 546–555 in `night-shift.sh`) with a call to the existing scoring infrastructure. Since `run_canary_eval` expects a static baseline, use `4.0` as the dynamic eval floor:

```bash
# Replace lines 546-554:
if [ -n "$DYN_PROMPT" ] && [ "$DYN_PROMPT" != "null" ]; then
  echo "$(date):   Scoring dynamic eval $((i+1)): ${DYN_PROMPT:0:60}..."
  run_canary_eval "developer" "$DYN_PROMPT" "DYN-$((i+1))" "4.0"
fi
```

The `run_canary_eval` function already handles: running the agent, calling the judge, parsing the score, writing the scored JSON line, and appending to FINDINGS if degraded. No new infrastructure needed.

**Impact:** Dynamic evals become real signal. Instead of 2 scored canaries per night (developer TC1, chief-of-staff TC1), we get 4. Regressions in recently-worked domains (agents, dashboard, hooks) are now catchable the same night they emerge.

**Risk:** `run_canary_eval` uses a fixed 5-criteria scoring rubric against the developer eval file — dynamic prompts may not map cleanly to all 5 criteria, creating noise in per-criteria scores. Mitigation: interpret dynamic evals by overall avg only (not per-criterion), which the function already supports.

---

## P-2026-04-08-002

**Category:** `self_improvement`

**Problem: Dynamic eval domain selection ignores skill gaps — highest-severity gap domain "hooks" has zero eval coverage**

Phase B-Dynamic selects domains from `domain_frequency_7d[:2]` (line 513 of `night-shift.sh`): the two most-used domains this week. Tonight that produced `agents, dashboard` — generating DYN-1 (retry mechanism) and DYN-2 (responsive layout). Both reasonable. Neither touches hooks.

Meanwhile, `skill-gaps.json` flags **hooks** as **high severity**, with evidence spanning two sessions, three memory clusters (`feedback_hook ×2`, `feedback_hooks ×1`, `feedback_pretooluse ×1`), and 10 hook_catches in 7 days. The developer agent is reliably encountering this domain but the eval suite has no coverage for it. We won't know if the agent's hook handling is improving or degrading because we're never testing it.

The two data sources — domain frequency and skill gap severity — exist in the same `$OUT_DIR` directory and are never cross-referenced.

**Solution:** Modify the domain selection block (lines 513–517 in `night-shift.sh`) to blend frequency with gap severity. Add the top skill gap domain to the generation prompt when gaps exist:

```bash
# After line 513:
TOP_DOMAINS=$(jq -r '.domain_frequency_7d[:2][]?.domain // empty' "$OUT_DIR/log-analysis.json" 2>/dev/null)
GAP_DOMAIN=$(jq -r '.gaps[0].domain // empty' "$OUT_DIR/skill-gaps.json" 2>/dev/null)

# Inject gap domain into the list if it's not already present and a gap was detected
if [ -n "$GAP_DOMAIN" ] && ! echo "$TOP_DOMAINS" | grep -q "$GAP_DOMAIN"; then
  TOP_DOMAINS=$(printf "%s\n%s" "$TOP_DOMAINS" "$GAP_DOMAIN" | head -3)
fi
```

Also update the generation prompt (line 520) to distinguish eval purpose: "1 prompt testing $GAP_DOMAIN edge-case awareness, 1 prompt testing $DOMAIN_LIST task quality."

**Impact:** Each night generates at least one eval in the domain where the agent is currently weakest. Skill gaps that are detected by B2 automatically get eval coverage in the same run — closing the loop between gap detection and eval validation. Over time, the eval suite naturally grows toward the system's real weaknesses rather than its most-frequent activities.

**Risk:** If the skill gap domain is something abstract (e.g., "external-data-access"), the prompt generator may produce vague evals. Mitigation: keep the frequency-based prompts as the primary pair and treat the gap-domain prompt as a third bonus eval, only appended when `severity == "high"`.

---

## P-2026-04-08-003

**Category:** `agent_methodology`

**Problem: Developer agent re-learns the same hook edge cases from scratch every session — fixes exist in memory but not in operating context**

Skill gap analysis (high severity, `skill-gaps.json` line 4–8) identifies two specific, recurring hook failures:
1. **Stop hook infinite loop** — triggered when the developer agent's own tool calls satisfy the Stop hook condition
2. **PreToolUse false-blocking of newly created files** — hook fires on files that don't exist yet, blocking creation

These have appeared in two separate sessions (`night-shift-build`, `night-shift-learning`). Memory clusters `feedback_hook (×2)`, `feedback_hooks (×1)`, and `feedback_pretooluse (×1)` confirm the lessons were learned — but they exist as scattered feedback files, not as operational guidance in the developer agent's working context. The agent encounters these edge cases fresh each session, re-solves them mid-session, creates another feedback memory, and the cycle repeats.

This is the root cause behind developer's 2/5 friction sessions and 10 hook_catches this week. The knowledge exists in the system; it's just not in the right place.

**Solution:** Add a `## Hook Edge Cases` section to `/Users/workshop/.claude/agents/developer.md` that encodes both failure modes as pre-task awareness:

```markdown
## Hook Edge Cases

Before writing any hook-adjacent code, be aware of two known failure patterns:

**1. Stop hook infinite loop**
- Cause: The Stop hook condition matches on tool calls made during hook resolution itself
- Prevention: Scope Stop hook triggers to user-turn events only, not internal tool calls. Add a `HOOK_RUNNING` env guard to prevent re-entrant execution.
- Test: After writing a Stop hook, verify it cannot be triggered by its own Claude invocation.

**2. PreToolUse false-blocking of new files**
- Cause: PreToolUse hook fires before `Write` on a path that doesn't exist yet; existence checks fail
- Prevention: Exempt paths from PreToolUse blocking when the tool is `Write` and the file does not currently exist (`[ ! -f "$FILE" ]`)
- Test: Verify the hook allows first-write to a new path before enforcing read-before-write rules.
```

These are permanent, structural lessons — not project-specific. They belong in the agent definition, not in transient memory.

**Impact:** Developer agent stops re-discovering these edge cases mid-session. Friction attribution in the hooks domain trends down. Hook_catches that are caused by loop/blocking bugs (the majority this week) stop occurring. The 2 feedback_hook memory clusters stop growing, because the lesson is already in operating context.

**Risk:** If hook behavior changes in a future Claude Code release, the documented edge case guidance could become stale. Mitigation: note the Claude Code version at time of documentation (`# Verified: Claude Code ~2026-04`), and flag for review if hook-related friction re-emerges after an update.

---

## P-2026-04-08-004

**Category:** `memory_consolidation`

**Problem: Memory consolidation phase never finds candidates — threshold-based detection misses semantically related clusters with different key prefixes**

`memory-trends.json` shows 115 memories with every single cluster flagged `"stable"` — no consolidation candidates surfaced tonight or any prior night. The consolidation logic fires on `count ≥ N within a single prefix`. But the hook knowledge is distributed across **three separate prefixes** that the detector treats as unrelated:

| Prefix | Count | Flag |
|--------|-------|------|
| `feedback_hook` | 2 | stable |
| `feedback_hooks` | 1 | stable |
| `feedback_pretooluse` | 1 | stable |

These four memories all address the same operational topic (Claude hook system edge cases). Individually, none crosses the consolidation threshold. Together, they're a clear candidate for synthesis into `reference_hooks.md` — a document that would immediately reduce per-session re-learning (see P-003 above).

The same pattern applies to the external-data cluster: `feedback_webflow (×3)` and `feedback_verify (×3)` are semantically adjacent (both concern unreliable external source access) but live under different prefixes.

The consolidation phase is blind to this because it operates on prefix-by-prefix counts with no semantic grouping pass.

**Solution:** Add a second consolidation detection pass to the memory trends phase in `night-shift.sh`. After the per-prefix count analysis, run a keyword overlap check across all `feedback_*` prefixes to identify clusters that share a meaningful substring:

```bash
# After existing per-prefix analysis:
# Group feedback_ prefixes by shared keyword (strip "feedback_" and look for overlap)
FEEDBACK_KEYS=$(echo "$CLUSTERS" | jq -r '.[] | select(.prefix | startswith("feedback_")) | .prefix' 2>/dev/null)

# Flag groups where 2+ prefixes share a root keyword OR where combined count ≥ 4
# e.g., feedback_hook + feedback_hooks + feedback_pretooluse → root "hook"
# Output as consolidation candidates with suggested reference key
```

In practice, the simplest reliable version: flag any set of `feedback_*` prefixes where stripping `feedback_` leaves a word that is a **prefix or substring** of another cluster's stripped name, and the combined count ≥ 3. This catches `hook`/`hooks`/`pretooluse` (where "hook" is root) without requiring LLM involvement.

The output should surface to the briefing as: *"Consolidation candidate: feedback_hook + feedback_hooks + feedback_pretooluse (combined: 4, topic: hooks) → suggest reference_hooks.md"*

**Impact:** Consolidation phase starts finding real candidates after being blind for (apparently) the system's entire run history. The hook-related memories get synthesized into a single reference document, reducing context load and ending the re-learning cycle. The approach generalizes to any topic where memories accumulate under variant prefixes.

**Risk:** Substring matching may produce false groupings (e.g., `feedback_agent` and `feedback_agency` would match on "agent"). Mitigation: require combined count ≥ 4 before flagging, and present candidates as human-review suggestions rather than automatic merges — the same model as the existing consolidation flow.

## Proposal Track Record
8 total, 4 accepted, 4 pending

## Benchmarks
| Benchmark | Status | Detail |
|-----------|--------|--------|
| Friction Rate | OK | 0 fix_attempts (14d) |
| Proposal Hit Rate | ?% | 4/4 accepted |
| Eval Signal | OK | 1 consecutive all-pass runs |

## Action Required: NO

skillGaps.evidence: Stop hook infinite loop and PreToolUse false-blocking of newly created files each appeared in two separate sessions (night-shift-build, night-shift-learning). Developer agent carries friction attribution in 2 of 5 sessions, and memory clusters feedback_hook (×2) and feedback_pretooluse (×1) confirm repeated re-learning of the same edge cases rather than durable fixes.
skillGaps.suggestion: Encode known hook edge cases (loop-prevention scoping, new-file exemption logic) into a shared hook configuration layer or spec document so the developer agent no longer encounters them cold each session — the fixes exist but aren't being retained operationally.

skillGaps.evidence: Research agent earned a friction attribution in career-identity when LinkedIn returned HTTP 999 (bot-block), forcing improvised screenshot workarounds. Memory clusters feedback_webflow (×3) and feedback_verify (×3) suggest repeated uncertainty about which external sources are reachable, pointing to a systemic gap rather than a one-off incident.
skillGaps.suggestion: Give the research agent an explicit fallback ladder for blocked domains (cached snapshots, alternative sources, structured screenshot escalation) so workarounds are codified protocol rather than improvised — and add a known-blocked-domains reference memory to prevent repeated re-discovery.

skillGaps.evidence: Bash 3.2 on the Mac Mini lacks associative arrays, requiring a full awk rewrite mid-session in night-shift-learning; a username mismatch required a sed fix. Bash, cron, and infrastructure domains appear across two recent sessions but no agent is designated for environment-compatibility concerns. feedback_bash32 is an isolated memory cluster, suggesting the lesson isn't propagating.
skillGaps.suggestion: Add a pre-execution environment checklist to the developer agent's context (target shell version, machine-specific usernames, PATH assumptions) so bash scripts default to POSIX-safe patterns and environment mismatches surface before runtime rather than causing mid-session rewrites.
